experiences.intentions:
Player 2: No role-specific hidden info as a Servant. On round 1, team [0,2] was proposed by me and includes myself; with no evidence against 0, approving a standard opening 2-person team is best. Approving reveals little and helps avoid unnecessary early rejection.
Player 0: I have no concrete role information yet; as a good Servant on round 1, a team including myself and the proposer is a standard low-information opener. Rejecting without evidence gives little benefit and risks wasting rounds. Approving reveals quest-result information without strongly harming good, since only one fail is needed on quest 1 and we need data.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil, so 0 and 2 are the only non-seen-evil players besides me. On quest 1, a 2-person team of 0 and 2 excludes known evil and also excludes me, which is generally a strong safe approve as Merlin because it avoids exposing myself while likely securing a pass. Approving reveals little beyond a reasonable preference for a clean team, which helps Good more than it hurts.
Player 4: I know player 1 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 3 are good-aligned roles. The proposed team [0,2] contains no evil players, so if approved it likely succeeds and gives good an early clean result. In round 1 as evil Minion, the optimal play is usually to reject all-good teams to increase chances of inserting evil later and to avoid confirming good pairs. My vote itself reveals limited information since early rejections are common, and rejecting helps evil more than approving this clean team.
Player 1: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 3 are not confirmed good individually, but only one of them can be the other evil. The proposed team [0,2] contains no known evil, so if approved it is likely an all-good team and Quest 1 would likely pass, which gives good side momentum and some trust signals. As evil on round 1, optimal play is often to reject teams that exclude all known evil, since approving helps a likely successful good quest with little upside. My vote reveals limited information because early rejections are common; rejecting a team without me or 4 is consistent with evil play but not too exposing in round 1.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are Evil, so team [0,2,3] contains no known Evil and is the strongest available 3-player team based on current info. We already had a successful [2,0] quest, and adding myself is safe. As Merlin, I should support good teams while not acting in a way that too blatantly exposes hidden knowledge; approving this team is natural given prior success and helps Good secure quest 2.
Player 0: Quest 1 succeeded with team [2,0], so both 0 (me) and 2 look somewhat trustworthy, though a single evil could have passed. Player 3 now adds themselves to [0,2,3]; with limited evidence this is a reasonable exploratory team and includes the two known-success participants. As a Good servant in team voting, approving solid teams that preserve successful members is optimal. Approving reveals little beyond normal confidence and helps avoid unnecessary rejection cycles.
Player 2: I know the previous successful 2-person team was [0,2], so both 0 and 2 look somewhat good, though one could theoretically be evil and choose success. Adding 3 is reasonable for quest 2 to test new information. As a Good Servant in team voting, I should generally approve teams containing members from a prior success unless there is stronger suspicion. Approving reveals willingness to build off confirmed-success composition, which helps Good gather information.
Player 4: I am Evil (Minion) and know player 1 is my teammate. The proposed team [0,2,3] contains no known Evil players, so it is likely all Good. As Evil in team voting, I generally want to reject likely-good teams to prevent Good from gaining another safe success and to create uncertainty. Rejecting also aligns with keeping my teammate off teams and can be justified because the previous successful team was [2,0], so adding 3 introduces uncertainty. My vote reveals little since mixed voting is common.
Player 1: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, so team [0,2,3] contains no evil players if roles are as seen. The previous successful team [2,0] also excluded evil, which slightly clears 0 and 2 in others' eyes. As evil in a team voting phase, I want to avoid approving an all-good team that is likely to succeed and further strengthen trust among good players. Rejecting also avoids giving this team legitimacy, and with only round 1 there is plenty of room to force alternative teams that may include me or 4 later. My vote itself reveals little since mixed voting is common; rejecting a good-looking team is slightly suspicious but worth it to hinder good progress.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know player 1 is Evil; player 4 proposed a team containing player 1. The two successful quests strongly suggest 0 and 2 are good, and excluding both proven-good players for a 2-person team while including a known evil is very bad for Good. In team voting, I should reject this team to prevent an evil-inclusive quest from going through. My vote aligns with good play and doesn't reveal too much beyond normal suspicion of a bad team.
Player 0: I am Good Servant. Two successful quests strongly suggest players 0 and 2 are likely good since both teams containing them succeeded with zero fails. Player 1 is less confirmed, while player 3 was also on a successful 3-person quest and looks somewhat good. The proposed team [0,1] drops both previously successful teammates 2 and 3 for quest 3, which is unnecessarily risky. As Good, optimal play is to favor teams built from proven successful players. Rejecting preserves information and pressures leaders to pick safer teams. My vote reveals preference for consistency with confirmed-success members, which helps Good.
Player 1: I know 4 is my evil teammate. Team [0,1] includes me but not 4. Players 0 and 2 have both been on successful teams, so approving this risks another clean success if I pass; failing would expose me strongly on a 2-person team after two clean quests. As evil, down 0-2, I want to avoid a likely good team passing while preserving cover. On team voting, rejecting is optimal: it may force a different team composition that includes 4 or creates more suspicion/disagreement, and my reject reveals little because evil commonly oppose good-looking teams.
Player 2: Quests 1 and 2 both succeeded with teams containing 0 and 2; I know I am Good, so player 0 looks strongly Good. Player 1 has no direct evidence yet, while player 3 was on a successful quest too but is excluded. For quest 3 in 5-player Avalon, a 2-person team is normal and we only need one fail to lose, so safest is usually to keep proven-success combinations. This proposed team [0,1] drops two previously successful members from the last quest and includes an untested player, creating unnecessary risk. Rejecting preserves pressure to form a more trusted team like [0,2] or [0,2,3]. My vote signals preference for evidence-based teams, which helps Good more than approving a speculative team.
Player 4: I know player 1 is my evil teammate; players 0 and 2 were on the first successful quest, and 0,2,3 were on the second successful quest, so at least several goods are among them, but success without fails does not clear anyone because evil can choose to pass. Current phase is team voting on my proposed team [0,1]. As evil down 0-2, getting an evil player onto quest 3 is critical, and this 2-person team with my teammate gives us a sabotage opportunity if approved. Voting approve supports evil win chances. My approve is not especially revealing since leaders often support their own team, and rejecting would undermine an evil-inclusive team.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: Two successful quests included players 0 and 2 both times, making 0 and 2 the strongest good candidates. As a Servant in team voting on quest 3, approving a proven-success team is optimal; rejecting risks unnecessary uncertainty. My vote reveals confidence in the cleanest available pair, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil, so 0 and 2 are very likely good based on both prior successful quests. The current phase is team voting on a 2-player team [0,2], and approving preserves a strong all-good team with a high chance of another success. Approving this team is also low-information and does not expose my knowledge; rejecting a proven-good team would be suspicious and could hurt Good.
Player 2: Two successful quests both included players 0 and 2, making 0 and myself the strongest good-leaning pair. Proposed team [0,2] repeats the exact pair that already succeeded and on quest 3 in a 5-player game a 2-person team is standard. As a good Servant, approving a proven successful team is optimal. My vote reveals consistency with evidence-based play and helps good more than it hurts.
Player 1: I know 4 is my evil teammate, so 0 and 2 are currently the core publicly trusted players because both successful quests included them. As evil and down 0-2, I want to stop another success. This is a team vote on [0,2], a highly good-coded pair with no evil on it, so if it goes it likely succeeds and puts good at match point. Rejecting is optimal. My reject aligns with evil interests and does not reveal much because some good players may also reject small teams or reconsider trust patterns.
Player 4: Players 0 and 2 have been on both successful quests, so they look broadly good to the table, but as evil I know at least one evil (me or 1) has been excluded so far. Team [0,2] repeats the exact pair that just succeeded; approving would lock in a highly trusted all-good pair and risks giving Good their third success. As Minion on team voting, optimal play is to reject strong good-looking teams, especially when evil is down 0-2. My reject is somewhat expected since I’m likely viewed with some suspicion after proposing [0,1], but preserving evil’s win chances matters more than concealment here.
